subject.title: Mathematics Applications & Interpretation (AI)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics: Applications and Interpretation** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **real-world applications of mathematics, statistical analysis, mathematical modeling, and data interpretation**, with a strong focus on **technology use, practical problem-solving, and interpreting mathematical results in context**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | External examination (calculator permitted) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 2** | External examination (technology required) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | External examination (technology required) | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal assessment | 30h | 30h | 20% |

strengths: Rough chronological structure with a clear introduction and conclusion
Use of headings, tables, and graphs to guide the reader
weaknesses: Frequent digressions and verbosity interrupt logical flow
Substantial repetition of definitions and equations
Basic organisation lacks refinement and polished coherence
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place coherence and structure at band 2 but conciseness at band 1. Overall the work shows some logical direction but is hindered by repetition and off-topic material, matching a low band. A score of 1/4 reflects limited coherence and organisation with significant irrelevant content.

strengths: Correct statement of basic regression formulas and function forms
Inclusion of multiple representations (tables, scatter plots, regression curves, transition matrix)
Descriptions of results are generally understandable
weaknesses: Inconsistent notation (e.g., R vs r) and missing units on tables and axes
Poorly labelled, low-resolution graphs without grids or scales
Conflicting numerical values (cubic coefficients) and notation slips
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands sit at band 2: some appropriate mathematical language and notation, some representations, and mostly clear communication with inconsistencies. A score of 2/4 reflects this middling performance in clarity, notation and effective representation.

strengths: Personal context (family ties to Scotland) adds authenticity and motivation
Choice to progress through multiple regression models shows some initiative
weaknesses: Model-selection follows standard classroom methods with no novel data processing
Limited evidence of independent thinking beyond routine approaches
Creativity and initiative confined to basic use of calculator functions
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All strands are at band 1: only some evidence of independent thinking, personal approach and creativity. The studentâs motivation is clear but mathematical choices remain conventional, warranting 1/3 for personal engagement.

strengths: Awareness of external factors (e.g., migration) shows basic reflection on model limitations
weaknesses: Reflections remain surface-level with limited critique of assumptions or parameter sensitivity
Connections to deeper understanding of demographics are minimal
Critical analysis is confined to comparing rÂ² values without probing sources of error
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments are band 1 throughout: limited depth, basic analysis, and only a minimal link between results and understanding. A 1/3 reflects the presence of reflection but its lack of substantive critical insight.

strengths: Application of a range of HL-level models (linear, exponential, logistic, Markov) relevant to the syllabus
Regression and substitution steps are logically structured
Demonstrates understanding of incremental model complexity
weaknesses: Numerical inaccuracies and misprints (e.g., incorrect coefficients, wrong logarithm derivation)
Misapplication of growth-rate formulas and confusion of r and rÂ²
Errors in logistic parameter signs and transition table formatting
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Relevance of mathematics is band 3 (course-level models used appropriately), but accuracy and understanding sit at band 2. Allocating 3/6 recognises the solid application of relevant HL mathematics tempered by repeated errors and partial conceptual grasp.

strandSelections.reasoning: Choosing Scotland because of personal family ties shows some individual motivation, yet model selection follows standard classroom techniques without novel data processing or parameter estimation. Evidence of independent thinking is therefore limited.

The student shows some conceptual grasp of what correlation, carrying capacity and transition matrices mean, but confuses r and rÂ² and misapplies exponential growth. Understanding is therefore âsomeâ rather than good or thorough.

Basic calculations (slope, r, logistic substitution) are generally correct, yet multiple numerical inaccuracies occur (incorrect cubic coefficients, growth-rate interpretation of 0.82 = 82 %). Such partial correctness corresponds to band 2.

The narrative contains substantial repetition (linear-model rationale explained twice; identical cubic equations re-listed; prolonged textbook-style definitions). Several paragraphs discuss well-known ecological concepts without linking them back to calculations. Such verbosity and off-topic content show limited conciseness, so the strand sits in band 1.

Basic regression notation (y = ax + b, r, rÂ²) and function forms are stated correctly, yet symbols are sometimes misused (upper-case R for correlation, inconsistent subscripts) and units are omitted. Hence there is âsome appropriateâ rather than âmostly appropriateâ mathematical language.

Descriptions of results are understandable, but notation slips (mixed comma/point separators, incorrect exponential constant) and occasional conflicting numbers (two cubic coefficients) create inconsistency. Thus the strand is âmostly clear with some consistencyâ rather than fully consistent.

Regression, exponential, logistic and Markov models are all part of the HL AI syllabus and are applied appropriately to real population data, satisfying the ârelevant mathematics at course levelâ criterion. The work is not sophisticated or highly efficient, so band 3 is the best fit.

Tables of raw data, scatter plots, regression curves, and a transition matrix are supplied, demonstrating more than minimal representation. However, axes are poorly labelled, images are low resolution, and no scale or grid is provided, limiting their effectiveness. This aligns with band 2: âsome appropriateâ representations.

The student comments on why certain models are unrealistic and mentions external factors such as migration, but reflections remain surface-level (e.g., simply stating âthis is not realisticâ). Hence reflection is present but limited.

While multiple models are tried, each is implemented via a calculatorâs regression function; no original coding, parameter tuning or alternative datasets are attempted. Creativity and initiative are therefore limited.

The student follows a roughly chronological structureâintroduction, successive modelling attempts, and conclusionâwhich gives the report some logical direction. Nevertheless, frequent digressions (e.g., lengthy definitions of carrying capacity or environmental factors) interrupt the argument and weaken the causal flow between sections. Overall coherence is present but uneven, so the work best fits a "some coherence and logical development" description.

Critical analysis is confined to comparing rÂ² values and declaring models âbetterâ or âworseâ. There is little probing of assumptions, parameter sensitivity or error sources. This meets the descriptor for basic analysis with limited critique.

Links between the mathematical results and the studentâs understanding of Scotlandâs demographics are mentioned but not explored deeply (e.g., no quantitative discussion of policy implications). The connection exists but remains limited.

Headings, tables and graphs help the reader navigate, and a clear introduction/conclusion are provided. However, tables sometimes repeat the same information, graphics are scattered without uniform styling, and page references (e.g., {10}â{16}) break the reading flow. These features indicate basic but not polished organisation, matching the âadequate organisation with basic structureâ level.

The investigation adheres to a textbook progression of models (linear â quadratic â cubic etc.) rather than a distinctly personal strategy. The studentâs comments on visiting grandparents add context but do not drive the mathematical choices, so only âsome evidenceâ of a personal approach is visible.

annotations.comment: The subtitle describes the topic but lacks a clear research question. Consider adding a precise question such as: âWhich mathematical model best predicts Scotlandâs population trend from 1980 to 2020?â
The personal context engages the reader and demonstrates genuine motivation. It adds authenticity by linking family ties to the chosen topic.
The objective statement is general. Strengthen clarity by explicitly stating hypotheses or criteria for model comparison (e.g., rÂ² threshold or error metrics).
Table 1 presents raw population figures but omits units on the header (âin Millionsâ vs raw counts). Clearly label units and consider converting to millions for readability.
Introducing linear regression first is logical and shows understanding of incremental model complexity.
Figure 1 lacks axis labels, units and gridlines, reducing interpretability. Ensure scatter plots display clearly labeled axes and scales.

The linear growth rate formula $$R=\frac{ÎP}{P_0\times t}$$ is unconventional; it calculates average annual percentage rather than true exponential rate. Consider using $$r=\frac{1}{t}\ln\frac{P_t}{P_0}$$.
The student reflects on external factors affecting linear growth, showing awareness of model limitations.
A residual plot or error analysis is missing for the quadratic regression. Including this would substantiate the claimed superiority over linear.
Notation is inconsistent: correlation sometimes denoted R, other times r. Adopt a single symbol (e.g., r) and define it clearly.
Testing f(40) for the quadratic model yields 25 million, which is far from reality. Emphasize domain constraints and avoid using the model for long-term prediction.
The student shows initiative by progressing to cubic regression to capture inflection points, demonstrating personal engagement.
The cubic coefficients differ (-0.258 vs -0.262). This inconsistency should be reconciled and precise values reported.

The title is clear but could be more descriptive by explicitly stating the investigationâs focus, for example: âEvaluating Population Growth Models for Scotland (1980â2020)â. This helps the reader immediately understand the scope.
Interpreting r=0.709 as ânot strongâ is valid, but clarify terms: 0.7 indicates moderate correlation. Relate rÂ²=0.503 to percentage of variance explained.
Equation in Fig 3 is misprinted as â19 â 3xÂ²â. Ensure the correct coefficient (19.3) and proper formatting (19.3xÂ²).
While f(10)=5.64 million approximates the 1990 value, the 560 000 difference (~11%) is substantial. Discuss error magnitude and implications.
The derivation of $$\ln(P(t))=\ln(P_0)\cdot r\cdot t\cdot \ln(e)$$ is incorrect. Use $$\ln(P(t))=\ln(P_0)+rt$$ based on logarithm properties.
Logarithm table omits the ln(P) value for 2020. Include this entry to maintain data consistency and complete regression input.
Axes on the transformed scatter plot (Fig. 4) are unlabeled in the OCR; ensure $$x$$ represents t and $$y$$ represents $$\ln(P)$$ for clarity.
The logistic parameter list states âe = base (-2.71828)â incorrectly. Eulerâs number is positive 2.71828 without a negative sign.
Carrying capacity assumptions (K = 8 million) would benefit from justification using historical population limits or resource data to support your estimate.
State intervals (S1âS5) overlap at boundaries and S4 uses a hyphen instead of a bullet. Standardize interval notation (e.g., inclusive lower, exclusive upper).
Transition counts table uses a square root symbol (ââ2â) for a count of 2. Counts should be integer values without symbolic formatting.
Probability matrix entries show inconsistent formatting (overlines, bold). Present probabilities as plain decimals (e.g., 0.5, 0.25) for readability.
Multiplying the state vector by T yields [0,0,0,0,0], indicating an absorbing state with no outgoing transitions. Discuss absorbing states and implications for long-term prediction.
